title: Prime Computer — Industry Product Requirements Plan (Five-Year Plan, July 1981, COMPANY CONFIDENTIAL)
author: Peter Kastner & Others — Prime Computer Inc., Market Planning and Management Department
abstract: Internal Prime Computer Inc. five-year Industry Product Requirements Plan dated July 15, 1981 (COMPANY CONFIDENTIAL), authored by Peter Kastner of Prime's Market Planning and Management Department of Marketing Development with input from Prime market managers (the 'and Others' coauthors). The plan synthesizes vertical-market plans for Construction Engineering, Power Utilities, Manufacturing Engineering, Manufacturing Production, Banking, Government, and Energy (Petroleum) into a single statement of common product requirements for Prime's first half-of-the-1980s strategy. Forecasts product revenue from planned markets growing from $117.7M in 1980 (44% of corporate goal) to $1,193.1M in 1985 (81% of corporate goal). Covers seven System Use Categories (Computational, Timesharing/Program Development, CAD/CAM, Office Automation, Transaction Processing, Query, Prime Information / Small Business) crossed against five system price bands ($30-49K Rabbit through $300K+ Prime 850). Identifies five priority near-term product gaps: programmable parallel-channel interface (HPC-4 / MPC-4 Special), floating-point precision, COBOL at full ANSI intermediate level, FORTRAN 77 compile/runtime performance, and Hyperchannel. Recommends a $10K-$30K personal computer with integral Primenet networking before mid-1980s. Identifies four 'attack' market opportunities: local-area networks of terminals, distributed processing including personal/departmental systems, office automation, and CAD/CAM. The plan defines the product strategy that Kastner — then in Prime Market Planning — used to influence Prime's 1981-1985 development cycle, and is the principal Kastner-authored artifact from his Prime Computer (1979-1981) employer era.
importance_rationale: Primary Kastner-authored Prime Computer artifact from his Prime employer era (1979-1981) and the only known surviving multi-hundred-page strategic five-year plan synthesizing all of Prime's 1981 vertical market plans into a single product-requirements roadmap. Captures Kastner's voice as a market planner before his Stratus and DEC tenures and before the founding of Aberdeen Group. Encodes Prime's revenue projections, the seven System Use Categories framework, and the priority product-gap list against DEC VAX competition.
relevance_rationale: Specific 1981 product gaps (FORTRAN 77 speed, ANSI COBOL, Hyperchannel) are obsolete, but the framework — vertical-market revenue stack-up, System Use Categories crossed with price bands, 'attack market' identification, and the call for a $10-30K networked personal computer before mid-1980s — is structurally identical to product-strategy frameworks used in modern B2B software and SaaS planning. Foundational artifact for understanding the late-minicomputer-era product planning tradecraft.
prescience_rationale: Several major calls verified by subsequent history: (1) the imperative for a $10-30K networked personal computer / 'professional managerial workstation' with integral host access — verified by the Apollo Domain, Sun-1, IBM PC Network and ultimately the workstation/PC LAN era; (2) office automation as fastest-growing segment with productivity payoff — verified; (3) DEC VAX as 'the competitive benchmark indefinitely' — verified through 1990s; (4) inability of Prime to gain Fortune-1000 TP traction before 1984 — verified, Prime never broke through against IBM/Tandem/Stratus in F1000 OLTP; (5) the local-area-network-of-terminals attack market — verified by Ethernet/Token Ring; (6) software-shortage-driven need for programmer productivity tools (Unix Programmer's Workbench cited as model) — verified by the rise of IDE, source control, generators. The plan also missed several major shifts: it underestimated the speed of the IBM PC's commodity rise from 1983, did not foresee the open-systems / Unix workstation replacement of vertically integrated minicomputers, and overestimated Prime's ability to retain DEC VAX-class customers — Prime itself was acquired/broken up by 1992 (J.H. Whitney leveraged buyout, then Computervision spinoff).
